excerpts.name: 陳振孫序
excerpts.text:
https://zh.wikisource.org/zh-hant/%E5%AF%B6%E5%88%BB%E5%8F%A2%E7%B7%A8%E5%BA%8F


始歐陽兗公為《集古錄》，有卷秩次第，而無時世先後。趙德甫《金石錄》，迺自三代秦漢而下敘次之，而不著所在郡邑。及鄭漁仲作《系時》《系地》二錄，亦疏略弗備。其他如諸道石刻錄、訪碑之類，於所在詳矣，而考訂或缺焉。都人陳思，儥書於都市，士之好古博雅，蒐遺獵忘以足其所，與夫故家之淪墜不振，出其所藏以求售者，往往交於其肆。且售且儥，久而所閱滋多，望之輒能別其真贋。一旦盡取諸家所錄，輯為一編，以今九域京府州縣為本，而繫其名物於左，昔人辨證審定之語具著之。既鋟本，首以遺余，求識其端。凡古刻所以貴重於世，歐陽公以來，言之悉矣，不待余言。余獨感夫古今宇宙之變，火焚水漂，陵隳谷堙，雖金石之堅，不足保恃。載祀悠緬，其毀勿存、存弗全者，不勝數矣。矧今河洛尚隔版圖，其幸而存且全可椎搨者，非邊牙市不可得。得或賈兼金，固不能家有而人見之也。則得是書而觀之，猶可想象彷彿於上下數千載間，其不謂之有補於斯文矣乎！思，市人也，其為是編，志於儥而已矣，而於斯文有補焉。視他書坊所刻，或蕪釀不切，徒費板墨、靡棕楮者，可同日語哉！誠以是獲厚利，亦善於擇術矣。余故樂為之書，是亦柳河東述宋清之意雲爾。紹定辛卯小至，直齋[[陳伯玉]]父。

《寶刻叢編》卷首。又見《六藝之一錄》卷八三二，《善本書室藏書志》卷一四，《萬卷精華樓藏書志》卷五八，《鄭堂讀書記》卷三三，《靜嘉堂秘藷志》卷二二。


**English Translation:**

Initially, Ouyang Xiu (Duke Yan of Ouyang)¹ created the _Jigu Lu_ (Record of Collected Antiquities), which was organized by volume and sequence but lacked chronological order regarding historical periods. Zhao Defu [Zhao Mingcheng]’s² _Jinshi Lu_ (Record of Metal and Stone Inscriptions) then arranged items chronologically from the Three Dynasties, Qin, and Han downwards, but did not indicate their locations in commanderies and counties. When Zheng Yuzhong [Zheng Qiao]³ produced his two records, _Xishi_ (Chronological Arrangement) and _Xidi_ (Geographical Arrangement), they were also sketchy and incomplete. Other works, like the _Zhudao Shike Lu_ (Records of Stone Inscriptions from Various Circuits) and the _Fangbei_ (Visiting Steles) genre, were detailed regarding locations but often lacked critical examination and verification.

Chen Si, a resident of the capital [Lin'an, modern Hangzhou], was a bookseller in the city market. Scholars devoted to antiquity and broad learning, seeking out lost and forgotten items to complete their collections, as well as members of fallen old families, bringing out their hidden treasures to sell, often converged at his shop. Buying and selling over a long time, he gradually saw more and more items, reaching the point where a mere glance allowed him to distinguish genuine artifacts from fakes. One day, he took all the records compiled by various scholars and edited them into a single collection. He used the contemporary administrative divisions of the Nine Regions—circuits (_jing_), prefectures (_fu_), subprefectures (_zhou_), and counties (_xian_)—as the framework, listed the inscribed objects under them, and fully included the analytical and evaluative comments of previous scholars. Once the book was engraved and printed, he first presented a copy to me (余), requesting that I write something for its beginning [a preface].

The reasons why ancient inscriptions are valued by the world have been exhaustively explained since Ouyang Xiu's time; they need no further words from me. I am uniquely moved by the transformations of the cosmos through past and present. Fire burns, water washes away, hills collapse, valleys fill in – even the hardness of metal and stone offers no reliable permanence. Through the long passage of years, those [inscriptions] destroyed and no longer existing, or existing but incomplete, are countless.

Moreover, the He-Luo region [North China, under Jurchen Jin rule at this time]⁴ is currently separated from our territory [Southern Song]. Those inscriptions fortunate enough to survive complete and be available for rubbing can only be obtained from border markets (_bianya shi_)⁵; obtaining one might cost a fortune (賈兼金, fetch double the gold), so certainly not every family can own one nor every person see one. Therefore, obtaining this book and viewing it allows one to still imagine and get a sense of [these inscriptions] across several thousand years. How can this not be considered a contribution to our culture (斯文, this culture/learning)?

Si is a man of the market (市人); in creating this compilation, his ambition was merely commerce (儥, selling/profiting). Yet, it contributes to our culture. Compared to what other bookshops engrave—often rambling, irrelevant, and merely wasting woodblocks, ink, palm fiber brushes, and paper—can they be spoken of in the same breath? If he indeed gains great profit from this, it is still a good choice of profession (善於擇術矣, skilled in choosing his art/method). Therefore, I am happy to write this for him. This is also in the spirit of Liu Hedong [Liu Zongyuan]⁶ writing about Song Qing⁷ – nothing more.

Written on the Winter Solstice (小至, _Xiaozhi_)⁸ of the Xin Mao year in the Shaoding era [December 16, 1231], by Zhizhai Chen Boyu Fu.

---

**Footnotes:**

1. **Ouyang Xiu (歐陽修, 1007-1072):** Courtesy name Yongshu (永叔), also known as Duke Yan of Ouyang (歐陽兗公). A preeminent scholar, historian, and statesman of the Northern Song dynasty, pioneer of epigraphic studies.
2. **Zhao Defu (趙德甫):** Courtesy name of Zhao Mingcheng 趙明誠 (1081-1129), a renowned epigrapher and antiquarian of the late Northern Song, husband of the famous poet Li Qingzhao.
3. **Zheng Yuzhong (鄭漁仲):** Courtesy name of Zheng Qiao (鄭樵, 1104-1162), a Southern Song historian known for his encyclopedic work _Tongzhi_ (通志).
4. **He-Luo region (河洛):** Refers to the area around the Yellow River (黃河) and Luo River (洛水), the traditional heartland of Chinese civilization, which was under the control of the Jurchen Jin dynasty during the Southern Song period when this preface was written.
5. **Border markets (邊牙市, _bianya shi_):** Likely refers to markets situated near the border between the Southern Song and Jin territories, or possibly brokers (_yá_ 牙 often refers to brokers) dealing in goods (including rubbings) from the north.
6. **Liu Hedong (柳河東):** Refers to Liu Zongyuan (柳宗元, 773-819), a famous Tang dynasty writer and official, known by his place of origin, Hedong.
7. **Song Qing (宋清):** A medicinal herb merchant praised by Liu Zongyuan in an essay for his integrity and ethical business practices, despite being a merchant, a traditionally lower-status profession. The writer uses this allusion to justify praising Chen Si, a bookseller, for his contribution to scholarship, even if driven by commercial motives.
8. **Winter Solstice (小至, _Xiaozhi_):** The term _Xiaozhi_ can sometimes refer to the day _before_ the actual Winter Solstice (_Dongzhi_ 冬至), but often it's used interchangeably or simply indicates the Winter Solstice period. Given the date format, it likely refers to the Winter Solstice itself. The Xin Mao year of the Shaoding era corresponds to 1231 CE.
